title: A Color Engine Built for the Brand, Not the Industry Default
subtitle: K-means perceptual color extraction that powers dynamic theming, card gradients, and WCAG-compliant text from content artwork
team: Design-led initiative with engineering partner, Berlin

hero_image.alt: Color extraction config UI: K-means clusters powering card gradients, text, and logo accent

sections.headline: A Color Engine Built for the Brand, Not the Industry Default
sections.subtext: Bibel TV needed dynamic color extraction that works in light mode, meets WCAG AA/AAA contrast, and feels warm rather than Hollywood dark. We built a K-means cluster engine in OKLCH perceptual color space that produces base color, accent color, text colors, and background gradients from a single content image.

images.alt: Config UI showing artwork with K-means extracted colors mapped to card gradient, text, logo accent, and page background
images.caption: From one artwork: base color, accent, two text colors, page gradient.

sections.quote: You can't just invert dark-mode logic. OKLCH color extraction is a different problem entirely.
sections.context: The core challenge: making dynamic color work from first principles

sections.headline: What the Engine Extracts
sections.content:
The K-means algorithm identifies the most prominent colors in any content artwork. But prominence alone isn't enough — we filter for hue and vibrance, then map clusters to specific design roles:

**Base color** — Used for card gradients that sit below and overlap the image. Guaranteed to produce AA or AAA contrast with text. This is the anchor of the theme.

**Accent color** — Extracted for the logo overlay and page background gradient. The gradient is composed of base color at 80% opacity over the background color. This creates the signature visual effect on the hero carousel.

**Two text colors** — Designed to enable multi-tone text treatments like Bibel TV's on-air broadcast rebrand. We ultimately simplified for the denser phone display, but having two available gives flexibility. On mobile, busy multi-text looks don't work — one high-contrast text color is enough. The second text color exists in the engine but stays unused by default.

**Page background gradient** — Computed from the base color at 80% opacity over the page background. Creates depth without overwhelming the content artwork.

sections.headline: Why Standard Extraction Doesn't Work
sections.content:
Most extraction tools (imgix, off-the-shelf libraries) work in RGB. They optimize for contrast against dark backgrounds. Flip to light mode, and the same extracted colors washed out text or feel too aggressive. You need perceptual control over lightness and chroma — something RGB and HSL don't give you.

OKLCH is a perceptually uniform color space. You can reason about lightness (L) and chroma (C) in ways that enable precise control over both light and dark mode themes simultaneously.

sections.headline: Key Design Decisions
sections.content:
**Design-led infrastructure** — I owned the color extraction architecture, not just the Figma design. This is unusual. Color extraction is a design decision embedded in code — making it requires understanding both perceptual color science and the brand's emotional relationship with its users.

**Light mode first** — We optimized for light mode correctness, then verified in dark mode. This is the reverse of streaming industry defaults.

**Simplified text colors for phone** — The API could support complex multi-text color schemes like the on-air brand. But on a dense mobile display, the extra text colors created visual noise. Sometimes the best design decision is not to use what you built. We kept the engine flexible but shipped with one text color default.

sections.headline: Technical Architecture
sections.content:
The color engine runs as part of the deployed API service. K-means clustering operates in RGB space, then converts to OKLCH for perceptual tuning. The algorithm:

1. Image loaded from content URL
2. K-means clustering identifies prominent colors
3. Hue and vibrance filtering removes dull/neutral clusters
4. Clusters mapped to roles: base color, accent, text 1, text 2
5. Contrast ratios calculated via WCAG algorithm
6. Config JSON returned with all theme values

The API serves configs as live data — artwork changes update the theme automatically, no code deploys.

metrics.label: Light Mode Preference
metrics.value: 87% of validation survey users preferred light mode (n=40)

metrics.label: Contrast Compliance
metrics.value: All extracted base colors produce AA or AAA compliant contrast with text

metrics.label: Delivery
metrics.value: API live and running. Config UI functional. Figma plugin integrated.

sections.headline: What I Learned
sections.content:
The biggest insight: infrastructure work is design work. The decisions embedded in the color extraction algorithm — hue thresholds, vibrance filters, contrast compliance — are deeply design decisions. When you delegate them to a generic API, you delegate brand quality.

The second: on mobile, restraint beats flexibility. The engine could support complex multi-text looks. But phone displays are dense — the extra text colors created noise, not depth. The API ships with capability it doesn't always use. That's fine. Sometimes the best design is knowing when not to deploy what you built.

sections.headline: Why This Matters for Your Team
sections.content: If your brand has specific visual requirements that industry tools don't match, the answer isn't to compromise the brand. It's to build the tool that does. I designed a color extraction engine because standard tools didn't solve the light-mode problem. Owning that infrastructure — from K-means clustering through OKLCH tuning through contrast compliance — is what makes the final product match the original vision.

executive.summary: Built a K-means color extraction API in OKLCH space that powers dynamic theming for Bibel TV's streaming app. 87% light mode user preference, full AA/AAA contrast compliance. Live and validated.
executive.angle: Design-led infrastructure: when industry defaults don't match your brand requirements

blog.summary: Every streaming app defaults to dark mode. We had to make premium work in light — for users who want warmth, not Hollywood. Here's how the color engine works.
blog.angle: The technical depth behind a design problem the industry hasn't solved

meta.source: Bibel TV project interview 2026-05-06 + comprehensive project case study + live API inspection
